nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving and still blackwater or clearwater tributaries and flooded forest streams; demersal, foraging over soft sandy or muddy substrates among leaf litter and root tangles

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (protects sensitive barbels during foraging)
leaf litter (dried Indian almond or oak leaves)
dense planting around tank edges for security

diet.notes: Opportunistic benthic forager; accepts sinking micro-pellets, catfish wafers, and sinking flake as staples. Relishes live and frozen foods — bloodworms, tubifex, daphnia, and brine shrimp. Should never be expected to survive on leftovers from other tank inhabitants; targeted bottom feeding is essential. Wild fish consume benthic worms, small crustaceans, insect larvae, and organic detritus.

avoidWith: aggressive bottom dwellers competing for territory
any fish large enough to swallow a 6 cm catfish

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase thumbnail; photographer credited as Hippocampus-Bildarchiv. License not explicitly stated on page. Full-resolution image may be available via FishBase photo gallery.

summary: A small, peaceful Peruvian Amazon catfish whose body is densely covered in small grey-brown spots. Best kept in groups of 6+ over fine sand. Frequently mislabelled in trade as C. punctatus or C. leucomelas; the 2024 Dias revision reassigns it to Brochis ambiacus.
careNotes: The Spotted Corydoras is an undemanding community fish well-suited to planted South American biotope setups. Groups of at least six are strongly recommended — solitary or paired individuals are visibly stressed and more prone to disease. A fine sand substrate is the single most important husbandry detail: coarse or sharp gravel abrades the sensitive barbels these fish rely on for foraging, leading to bacterial infections and rapid decline. Maintain a minimum 3 cm sand layer. Filtration should provide gentle flow; sponge filters or spray-bar outputs are ideal. Water quality must be consistent — Corydoradinae are sensitive to ammonia and nitrite spikes even when pH and temperature are within range. Perform regular 25–30% water changes weekly. The species is frequently mislabelled in the aquarium trade as Corydoras punctatus (now Hoplisoma punctatum) or C. leucomelas (now Hoplisoma leucomelas); ambiacus is distinguishable by its pattern of many small, evenly scattered grey spots over a beige-olive body, with the dorsal fin bearing a dark blotch that is largely absent from the white-tipped leading spine — distinguishing it from the overlapping ranges of agassizii and punctatus. The 2024 Dias et al. revision published in the Zoological Journal of the Linnean Society reassigns this species to Brochis ambiacus; most hobby sources and trade still use Corydoras ambiacus and both names appear in commerce. Like all Corydoradinae, the species possesses sharp, lockable pectoral and dorsal spines that can envenomate a handler — handle with a soft net, never bare hands or cupped palms. The venom is mildly painful and the wound commonly becomes inflamed; spines can break off in skin. The species is facultative air-breathers and will periodically dart to the surface to gulp atmospheric air, especially in low-oxygen or warm conditions — this is normal behaviour, not a distress indicator unless frequency becomes excessive.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is achievable but less reliably triggered than for C. aeneus or C. paleatus. Condition a group of at least two females per male on live and frozen foods for several weeks. A substantial (20–30%) cool water change mimicking the onset of Amazonian rains is the standard spawning trigger. Eggs are adhesive and deposited on broad plant leaves, glass, or driftwood surfaces. Remove adults after spawning or transfer eggs to a separate hatching container to prevent predation. Eggs hatch in 3–5 days at 24°C; fry are free-swimming within another 2–3 days and accept infusoria, micro-worms, and finely powdered fry food. Fungused eggs should be removed promptly. Breedingdifficulty is rated moderate — the species will spawn given correct conditioning and a trigger, but fry survival rates in community tanks without egg separation are typically low.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Corydoras ambiacus
primary.notes: Primary husbandry reference. Provided temperature 21–27°C, pH 6.0–8.0, hardness 36–215 ppm, adult size 60–65 mm SL, native range (Río Ampiyacu, Yavarí, Napo, Nanay, lower Ucayali basins in Peru; Pangayacu and Yasuní systems in Ecuador), diet, behaviour, and note that the species name is commonly misapplied to similar-looking fishes.

additional.name: FishBase — Brochis ambiacus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (4.9 cm SL), temperature (21–24°C), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (2–25 dH), native range (upper Amazon basin), taxonomy (Callichthyidae, Siluriformes), and facultative air-breathing note. Hardness converted: 2–25 dH ≈ gH 2–25; used gH 2–15 per SeriouslyFish softer-end Amazonian biotope context.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Cory Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General Corydoras care reference used for tank size (20 gal / ~75 L minimum for standard varieties), substrate guidance, grouping (6+), diet (worms, sinking wafers), and compatibility notes. No C. ambiacus-specific page exists; general genus data applied.

additional.name: Aquarium Glaser — Corydoras revised, new genus names
additional.notes: Used for the 2024 Dias et al. revision details: Corydoras ambiacus reassigned to Brochis ambiacus; C. punctatus to Hoplisoma punctatum; C. leucomelas to Hoplisoma leucomelas. Seven genera: Corydoras, Aspidoras, Scleromystax, Gastrodermus, Osteogaster, Brochis, Hoplisoma.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net — Spotted Cory (Corydoras ambiacus)
additional.notes: Secondary care reference. Confirmed temperature 21–25°C, pH 6.0–7.6, hardness 4–22 dH, adult size 4–5 cm, lifespan 3–5 years, omnivorous diet, peaceful shoaling behaviour, native range (Rio Ambyiacu, Peru). Noted breeding as rare in captivity with cool-water-change trigger.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer; deposits adhesive eggs on plant leaves and glass. Cool water-change trigger required. Separate eggs or adults post-spawn to protect fry. Hatch in 3–5 days; fry accept micro-worms and powdered fry food.
